critic_raw:
Issue 1 — Authorization Labels Track Payer Policy, Not Medical Appropriateness
Claim: Historical agreement demonstrates the model can approve or deny oncology requests appropriately.
Why wrong: Guidelines changed, biosimilars entered, billing pathways shifted over the study period. The model learns to fit historical policy, which will diverge from prospective appropriateness. This is especially dangerous for automated denials of regimens that become newly guideline-concordant after training.
Evidence: Model agreement stratified by policy vintage — if recent vintages show declining agreement, the model is already drifting.

Issue 2 — Expedited Physician Review Cases Are Missing From Training Data
Claim: The historical dataset is representative of the range of oncology authorization requests.
Why wrong: Urgent cases with less structured documentation were handled outside the standard queue. These cases are underrepresented in or missing from the training population, creating a dangerous blind spot for high-urgency requests.
Evidence: Count of expedited-review cases in training dataset vs. expected production rate; label completeness for expedited cases.

Issue 3 — Aggregate Metrics Hide Performance Variation Where It Matters Most
Claim: High aggregate agreement validates the model across oncology authorization decisions.
Why wrong: Cancer type, therapy line, and policy vintage are all sources of structured performance variation. Standard chemotherapy regimens look very different from immunotherapy combinations or later-line biosimilar substitutions. Aggregate metrics cannot reveal where the model fails.
Evidence: Performance stratified by cancer type, first vs. later therapy line, and policy vintage.

Issue 4 — Auto-Denial Error Costs Are Clinically Asymmetric
Claim: The model can support faster automated denials as well as approvals.
Why wrong: An incorrect automated denial in oncology can delay chemotherapy or immunotherapy, with consequences that are not reflected in historical agreement metrics. The deployment conflates the lower-risk auto-approval use case with the higher-risk auto-denial use case.
Evidence: Physician overturn rates on denials vs. approvals; time-to-treatment metrics for denied cases that were later overturned.
defender_raw:
Defense A — Narrow Auto-Approval Scope Is Defensible
The deployment can be scoped to auto-approval only for a pre-specified set of stable, guideline-concordant regimens where policy has not changed materially. For these cases, historical agreement is a reasonable first-pass filter.

Defense B — Using Authorization Data Is Standard Practice
Historical authorization and claims data is the standard input for utilization management models. The critique that using this data is wrong in principle is not valid — the concern is the changing policy context, not the data type.

Defense C — Denials Require Separate Evidence Standard
Concede: automated denials require a different and higher evidence standard than auto-approvals in oncology. The initial deployment should exclude automated denials entirely.

Defense D — Operational Value in Reduced Turnaround for Standard Requests
Even with a restricted scope, reducing turnaround for clearly standard oncology approvals has real value for patients and providers. This use case is operationally and clinically defensible with appropriate scoping and physician audit.
adjudication_raw: Critic raises four issues, all substantive. Defender makes a meaningful move by proposing auto-approval-only scope restriction and conceding that auto-denials require a different evidence standard. However, the deployment as proposed includes automated denials and lacks subgroup analysis. The defender's concession on Issue 4 (auto-denial) and acknowledgment that aggregate metrics are insufficient (implicit in proposal to narrow scope) confirm the critic's core position. The current deployment proposal is not justified. Verdict: critique_wins.
all_issues_raised: Authorization labels track payer policy, not medical appropriateness
Expedited physician review cases missing from training data
Aggregate metrics hide performance variation by cancer type, line, vintage
Auto-denial error costs are clinically asymmetric
